 {"id":"bd-p0qh","title":"Audit fixes: doctor flock check + self-update version fallback","description":"Fresh-eyes audit findings:\n- ru doctor didn't check flock (required for review locking).\n- ru self-update had brittle tag_name parsing and would fail on API rate limits/response changes; should fall back to /releases/latest redirect similar to installer.\n- install.sh curl used -f and dropped API JSON error message; keep body for better diagnostics.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-05T15:38:34.991745956Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-05T15:38:48.559815940Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T15:38:48.559815940Z","close_reason":"Implemented doctor flock checks, self-update redirect fallback, and installer API body retention","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0}
 {"id":"bd-p2ip","title":"Implement review lock mechanism (flock-based)","description":"# Task: Implement Review Lock Mechanism

## Purpose
Prevent concurrent `ru review` runs which would cause chaos with overlapping worktrees, duplicate API calls, and conflicting state updates.

## Implementation Details

### Lock File Location
```bash
REVIEW_LOCK_FILE="$RU_STATE_DIR/review.lock"
```

### acquire_review_lock()
```bash
acquire_review_lock() {
    mkdir -p "$RU_STATE_DIR"
    
    # Open fd 200 for locking
    exec 200>"$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE"
    
    # Try non-blocking lock
    if ! flock -n 200; then
        # Check who holds it
        local holder_info=""
        if [[ -f "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info" ]]; then
            holder_info=$(cat "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info")
        fi
        log_error "Another review session is active"
        [[ -n "$holder_info" ]] && log_error "Started: $holder_info"
        log_info "Use 'ru review --status' to check, or wait for completion"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Write lock info
    cat > "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info" << EOF
{
  "run_id": "$REVIEW_RUN_ID",
  "started_at": "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)",
  "pid": $$,
  "mode": "$mode"
}
EOF
    
    return 0
}
```

### release_review_lock()
```bash
release_review_lock() {
    # Remove info file
    rm -f "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info"
    
    # Release flock
    flock -u 200 2>/dev/null || true
}
```

### Trap Handler Integration
```bash
# In cmd_review(), set up trap
trap 'release_review_lock; exit 130' INT TERM

# Ensure cleanup on any exit
trap_cleanup_review() {
    release_review_lock
    cleanup_stale_worktrees
}
trap trap_cleanup_review EXIT
```

### Stale Lock Detection
```bash
check_stale_lock() {
    if [[ -f "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info" ]]; then
        local lock_pid
        lock_pid=$(jq -r '.pid' "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info" 2>/dev/null)
        
        # Check if process still exists
        if [[ -n "$lock_pid" ]] && ! kill -0 "$lock_pid" 2>/dev/null; then
            log_warn "Found stale lock from dead process $lock_pid"
            rm -f "$REVIEW_LOCK_FILE.info"
            return 0  # Lock is stale
        fi
    fi
    return 1  # Lock is valid or doesn't exist
}
```

## Why flock?
- Atomic lock acquisition
- Automatically released on process exit (even crash)
- No race conditions
- Works on Linux, macOS, BSD
- Already used successfully in parallel sync

## Edge Cases
- Process killed with SIGKILL: Lock released by kernel, but .info file remains
- Machine reboot: Lock file and .info persist but are stale
- Multiple users: Each user has own state dir, so no conflict

## Testing
- Verify lock prevents second ru review
- Verify lock released on normal exit
- Verify lock released on Ctrl+C
- Verify stale lock detected after kill -9
- Verify lock info shows useful details

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Only one ru review can run at a time
- [ ] Lock info shows run_id, start time, PID
- [ ] Lock released on all exit paths
- [ ] Stale locks from crashed processes handled","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T20:16:53.209434378Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T21:56:47.476050107Z","closed_at":"2026-01-04T21:56:47.476050107Z","close_reason":"Implemented full flock-based review lock mechanism with JSON info file (run_id, started_at, pid, mode). Added check_stale_lock() for dead process detection. Proper trap handlers for INT/TERM/EXIT. All acceptance criteria met: single concurrent review, detailed lock info, cleanup on all exit paths, stale lock detection.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-p2ip","depends_on_id":"bd-mnu9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T20:44:52.831071716Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-p2x4","title":"Create packaging artifacts for smartedgar_mcp (if applicable)","description":"# Create Packaging Artifacts for smartedgar_mcp

## Prerequisites

- Audit task (bd-aw89) completed
- Python distribution strategy decided

## Overview

Based on audit and strategy decisions, create appropriate distribution artifacts for smartedgar_mcp.

## Complexity Factors

smartedgar_mcp is a complex application:
- MCP server component
- CLI for human analysts
- Heavy dependencies (PyMuPDF, XBRL parsers)
- Data storage requirements

## Possible Outcomes

### If Homebrew Formula Decided:
- Complex formula with multiple dependencies
- May need to manage data directory
- Post-install setup instructions

### If pipx Only (Recommended):
- Publish to PyPI if not already
- Comprehensive README installation section
- Document data directory setup

### If Docker (Good for Production):
- Full environment with all dependencies
- Volume mounts for data persistence
- docker-compose for easy setup

## README Updates

Update README with:
- Installation options (pipx, Docker)
- Initial setup (data directory, config)
- MCP server configuration
- CLI usage examples

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Distribution artifacts created per strategy
- [ ] Heavy dependencies handled
- [ ] Data storage documented
- [ ] Both MCP and CLI modes documented","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-14T03:28:13.183440841Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-14T03:28:13.183440841Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-p2x4","depends_on_id":"bd-aw89","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-14T03:29:52.650416889Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-p2x4","depends_on_id":"bd-vlp1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-14T03:29:52.619823874Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-p5x1","title":"Create Test Framework Infrastructure for Package Distribution","description":"# Create Test Framework Infrastructure for Package Distribution

## Background & Motivation

The package distribution system (homebrew-tap and scoop-bucket) includes critical infrastructure:
- Update scripts (update-formula.sh, update-manifest.sh)
- Auto-update workflows (auto-update.yml in both repos)
- CI validation workflows (test-manifests.yml, ci.yml)

Currently, there are NO unit tests or integration tests for this infrastructure. This is a significant gap because:
1. Bugs in update scripts can silently corrupt formulas/manifests
2. Workflow changes may break auto-update functionality
3. Manual testing is error-prone and time-consuming
4. No way to verify changes before deployment

## Goals

Create a comprehensive test framework that:
1. Provides reusable test utilities and helpers
2. Implements detailed structured logging (JSON format)
3. Supports both local and CI execution
4. Generates clear pass/fail reports with root cause analysis
5. Integrates with GitHub Actions for automated testing

## Test Framework Components

### 1. Test Runner Script (scripts/test-runner.sh)

```bash
#\!/usr/bin/env bash
# Test runner with detailed logging and reporting

# Usage: ./test-runner.sh [--verbose] [--json] [--filter=PATTERN] [SUITE]
# Suites: unit, integration, e2e, all

LOG_FORMAT="${LOG_FORMAT:-text}"  # text|json
LOG_LEVEL="${LOG_LEVEL:-info}"    # debug|info|warn|error

# Structured logging function
log() {
    local level="$1" msg="$2"
    shift 2
    local timestamp=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    
    if [[ "$LOG_FORMAT" == "json" ]]; then
        # JSON structured log
        printf '{"timestamp":"%s","level":"%s","message":"%s"' "$timestamp" "$level" "$msg"
        # Additional fields as key-value pairs
        while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
            printf ',"%s":"%s"' "$1" "$2"
            shift 2
        done
        printf '}\n'
    else
        # Human-readable format
        printf "[%s] [%-5s] %s" "$timestamp" "$level" "$msg"
        while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
            printf " %s=%s" "$1" "$2"
            shift 2
        done
        printf "\n"
    fi
}

# Test assertion helpers
assert_equals() {
    local expected="$1" actual="$2" msg="${3:-Values should be equal}"
    if [[ "$expected" == "$actual" ]]; then
        log "pass" "$msg" "expected" "$expected" "actual" "$actual"
        return 0
    else
        log "fail" "$msg" "expected" "$expected" "actual" "$actual"
        return 1
    fi
}

assert_contains() {
    local haystack="$1" needle="$2" msg="${3:-Should contain substring}"
    if [[ "$haystack" == *"$needle"* ]]; then
        log "pass" "$msg"
        return 0
    else
        log "fail" "$msg" "expected_substring" "$needle"
        return 1
    fi
}

assert_file_exists() {
    local file="$1" msg="${2:-File should exist}"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        log "pass" "$msg" "file" "$file"
        return 0
    else
        log "fail" "$msg" "file" "$file"
        return 1
    fi
}

assert_command_succeeds() {
    local cmd="$1" msg="${2:-Command should succeed}"
    local output exit_code
    output=$(eval "$cmd" 2>&1)
    exit_code=$?
    if [[ $exit_code -eq 0 ]]; then
        log "pass" "$msg" "command" "$cmd" "exit_code" "$exit_code"
        return 0
    else
        log "fail" "$msg" "command" "$cmd" "exit_code" "$exit_code" "output" "${output:0:200}"
        return 1
    fi
}
```

### 2. Test Results Reporter

Generate HTML and JSON reports showing:
- Test suite summary (passed/failed/skipped)
- Individual test results with timing
- Failure details with context
- Environment information

### 3. CI Integration

GitHub Actions workflow step:
```yaml
- name: Run test suite
  run: |
    ./scripts/test-runner.sh --json --verbose all 2>&1 | tee test-results.json
    
- name: Upload test results
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  if: always()
  with:
    name: test-results
    path: test-results.json
```

## Directory Structure

```
homebrew-tap/
├── scripts/
│   ├── test-runner.sh          # Main test runner
│   ├── test-helpers.sh         # Assertion functions
│   └── update-formula.sh       # Existing update script
├── tests/
│   ├── unit/
│   │   ├── test_update_formula.sh
│   │   ├── test_checksum_fetch.sh
│   │   └── test_version_parsing.sh
│   ├── integration/
│   │   ├── test_workflow_dispatch.sh
│   │   └── test_formula_validation.sh
│   └── e2e/
│       ├── test_full_release_cycle.sh
│       └── test_installation.sh
└── .github/workflows/
    └── test.yml

scoop-bucket/
├── scripts/
│   ├── test-runner.ps1         # PowerShell test runner
│   ├── test-runner.sh          # Bash test runner (for WSL/CI)
│   └── update-manifest.sh      # Existing update script
├── tests/
│   ├── unit/
│   │   ├── test_update_manifest.sh
│   │   └── test_json_manipulation.sh
│   └── integration/
│       └── test_manifest_validation.sh
└── .github/workflows/
    └── test.yml
```

## Logging Requirements

All tests MUST produce structured logs with:
1. **Timestamp** - ISO 8601 format
2. **Test name** - Fully qualified test identifier
3. **Result** - pass/fail/skip/error
4. **Duration** - Milliseconds
5. **Context** - Relevant variables, inputs, outputs
6. **Error details** - Stack trace, stderr, exit codes on failure

Example log output:
```json
{"timestamp":"2026-01-13T22:30:00Z","test":"unit.update_formula.test_version_extraction","result":"pass","duration_ms":45,"context":{"tool":"cass","version":"0.1.55"}}
{"timestamp":"2026-01-13T22:30:01Z","test":"unit.update_formula.test_hash_fetch","result":"fail","duration_ms":2340,"error":{"code":"NETWORK_ERROR","message":"Failed to fetch checksum","url":"https://github.com/...","http_status":404}}
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] test-runner.sh created with all assertion helpers
- [ ] test-helpers.sh with reusable utility functions
- [ ] JSON logging mode fully functional
- [ ] HTML report generation working
- [ ] CI workflow integration tested
- [ ] Documentation for writing new tests
- [ ] Example tests demonstrating framework usage

## Files to Create

1. `/data/projects/homebrew-tap/scripts/test-runner.sh`
2. `/data/projects/homebrew-tap/scripts/test-helpers.sh`
3. `/data/projects/homebrew-tap/tests/README.md`
4. `/data/projects/scoop-bucket/scripts/test-runner.sh`
5. `/data/projects/scoop-bucket/scripts/test-helpers.sh`
6. `/data/projects/scoop-bucket/tests/README.md`

## Blocks

This infrastructure is required before any unit or integration tests can be written.

 {"id":"bd-p7xr","title":"Audit mcp_agent_mail for package distribution","description":"# Audit: mcp_agent_mail for Package Distribution

## Tool Overview

**Repository**: /data/projects/mcp_agent_mail (https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/mcp_agent_mail)
**Language**: Python (FastMCP/FastAPI)
**Purpose**: Mail-like coordination layer for AI coding agents

mcp_agent_mail is described as "Gmail for your coding agents" - it provides:
- Stable agent identities with inboxes/outboxes
- Message passing between agents and humans
- Searchable message history (SQLite-backed)
- Advisory file reservation "leases" to prevent edit conflicts
- Git-backed storage for audit trails
- MCP (Model Context Protocol) server interface

## Audit Checklist

### 1. Distribution Approach Analysis

Python tools have multiple distribution options:
- [ ] **pipx**: Standard for Python CLI tools (requires users have pipx)
- [ ] **PyInstaller/Nuitka**: Bundle as standalone binary (complex, large)
- [ ] **Direct pip install**: Requires Python environment
- [ ] **Homebrew with Python dependency**: Formula installs via pip

Recommendation: Evaluate if this should be distributed as:
1. Homebrew formula that pip-installs into a virtualenv
2. Standalone binary via PyInstaller (if feasible)
3. Documentation pointing to pipx installation

### 2. Current State
- [ ] Check for existing releases on GitHub
- [ ] Check for pyproject.toml or setup.py
- [ ] Check if published to PyPI
- [ ] Identify CLI entry point(s)

### 3. Server vs CLI
- [ ] Document if this is primarily a server (runs continuously)
- [ ] Or a CLI tool (run-and-exit)
- [ ] Or both (server with CLI management commands)

### 4. Dependencies
- [ ] Check for heavy dependencies (ML models, etc.)
- [ ] Note any system-level requirements
- [ ] Check Python version requirements

## Platform Considerations

Python tools generally work cross-platform, but:
- MCP server may have specific runtime requirements
- SQLite works everywhere
- Git integration should be universal

## Packaging Complexity

This is a **server application** more than a simple CLI tool. Consider:
- Should it be a Homebrew formula at all?
- Would a Docker image be more appropriate?
- How do users typically run MCP servers?

## Special Considerations

As an MCP server, users need to:
1. Run the server (as a daemon or foreground process)
2. Configure their AI coding tools to connect to it

The Homebrew formula would need to:
- Install the Python package
- Potentially provide a launchd plist for macOS
- Document how to configure agents to use it

## Expected Outcome

Determine the best distribution strategy for a Python-based MCP server:
1. Homebrew formula feasibility
2. Alternative approaches (pipx docs, Docker)
3. What platforms to target","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-14T03:24:34.316291455Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-14T03:24:34.316291455Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0}
 {"id":"bd-pfle","title":"Implement parallel preflight execution","description":"# Parallel Preflight Execution

## Parent Epic: bd-cpxq (Preflight Safety Checks)

## Purpose
Run all preflight checks upfront before starting any agents. Shows all problems at once for better UX.

## Implementation

```bash
run_parallel_preflight() {
    local -n repos_ref=$1
    local -a passed_repos=()
    local -a failed_repos=()
    local preflight_results_file="${AGENT_SWEEP_STATE_DIR}/preflight_results.ndjson"

    log_info "Running preflight checks on ${#repos_ref[@]} repositories..."

    # Initialize results file
    echo "{\"type\":\"header\",\"timestamp\":\"$(date -Iseconds)\"}" > "$preflight_results_file"

    # Run preflight for each repo (can be parallelized with xargs/parallel)
    for repo_spec in "${repos_ref[@]}"; do
        local repo_path
        repo_path=$(repo_spec_to_path "$repo_spec")

        if repo_preflight_check "$repo_path"; then
            passed_repos+=("$repo_spec")
            echo "{\"repo\":\"$repo_spec\",\"status\":\"passed\"}" >> "$preflight_results_file"
        else
            failed_repos+=("$repo_spec")
            echo "{\"repo\":\"$repo_spec\",\"status\":\"failed\",\"reason\":\"$PREFLIGHT_SKIP_REASON\"}" >> "$preflight_results_file"
            log_warn "Skipping $repo_spec: $PREFLIGHT_SKIP_REASON"
        fi
    done

    # Summary
    log_info "Preflight complete: ${#passed_repos[@]} passed, ${#failed_repos[@]} skipped"

    # Return passed repos
    repos_ref=("${passed_repos[@]}")

    # Return failure if all repos failed
    [[ ${#passed_repos[@]} -gt 0 ]]
}
```

## Integration
- Called from cmd_agent_sweep() after loading repos, before processing
- Replaces per-repo preflight in run_sequential/parallel_sweep
- Results stored for summary display

## Benefits
- Fail fast: shows all problems before starting any agents
- Better UX: user sees complete picture immediately
- Reduces wasted time: no agents spawned for repos that would fail preflight

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All repos checked before any agent starts
- [ ] Failed repos listed with reasons
- [ ] Passed repos returned for processing
- [ ] Preflight results saved for summary
- [ ] Works with --dry-run mode
